This seems to be fixed now:
- [sensitivity](https://glosario.carpentries.org/en/#sensitivity) is defined as True Positive rate
- [specificity](https://glosario.carpentries.org/en/#specificity) is defined as True Negative rate
both of which match the [wikipedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sensitivity_and_specificity) definitions.

I opened a PR #761 for crosslinking this term but I think this issue can be safely closed.

en:
term: Descriptive Statistic
def: >
information that describes or summarizes the characteristics of a data set, such as mean, standard deviation, variance, or sample size.

en:
term: Descriptive Statistics
def: >
process of using and analyzing a set of descriptive statistics. The purpose of descriptive statistics is to summarize a sample. This differs from inferential statistics which summarizes the population that the sample is thought to represent.

To clarify I think some aspects specific to each language will be necessary. If there is a style guide in English and some general points applying to all contributions that would be a helpful starting point though.

We've discussed some consistent style decisions amongst the contributors for Japanese. As the number of contributors increases, it would be good to have somewhere to document these guidelines for new contributors to avoid them feeling they made a mistake during reviews.

Have you considered how you document a style guide? We've been considering using "Read The Docs" (which supports multilingual versions) for other projects as used for this repository:

https://github.com/carpentries-i18n/i18n-handbook 
https://carpentries-i18n-handbook.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

It's definitely something to consider. We have also been approached by Crowdin for a similar type of integration and members of our localisation community use Transifex. 

At this moment, however, the Carpentries Core Team does not have the capacity to support any new integrations.

Thanks for bringing this up @bkmgit! Capacity within the Core Team is unfortunately an issue, but we are constantly looking for ways to better the platform. We will keep this issue open and reevaluate it quarterly.

I think this may not be so difficult to do for Crowdin, which can import [glossaries as CSV files](https://support.crowdin.com/enterprise/glossary/?q=glossary). So if the glosario yml file could be converted to CSV, it should work. The next step would be adding CI, not sure how that would work tho.

Some solutions use plugins that apparently don't play well with GitHub Pages, but this one says it doesn't require plugins: [Tipue](https://github.com/jekylltools/jekyll-tipue-search). It's archived, but might be a start?

Or there are Google site searches, which might be less pretty, but unarchived.

Hi, I'd be interested to help with this! I've used [Simple Jekyll Search](https://github.com/christian-fei/Simple-Jekyll-Search) before, I believe it works fine with GH Pages. It might require a bit of adaption as the searched objects are individual pages, but I'd imagine most of the code would be reusable (the repo contains both the minified release and the source code).
after 2020-01-08 mail from Bailey Harrington

@r-scotti Yes, this is basically it. Would you like to submit a Pull Request suggesting an improved version? The use of existing definitions (like the Wolfram one) has been discussed in Issue #166, so you could either submit their version, or one based on that. Either way, myself and possibly the other English editor will look over it and check the definition and formatting.

Hello @baileythegreen  I am not at all familiar with this way of collaborating, yet I would like to understand better how it works.
Using R I cloned the repo (should I have 'forked' it?). Then I opened a new branch (that could be done only locally, of course! is this step at all useful?)
Checking up "- slug: absolute_error" I realized that the English definition contents are shared by all the languages I could read.
It is evidently not a question of language, rather a question of concept definition.
[Wolframs definition](https://mathworld.wolfram.com/AbsoluteError.html#:~:text=The%20difference%20between%20the%20measured,sum%20of%20their%20absolute%20errors.) includes taking the absolute value as an option while current definition here presents the operation as a fixed characteristic. 
In my perception absolute error is opposed to relative error, the latter being the pure number given by the ratio between absolute error and the value in question. Taking the absolute value of the difference is not necessarily a useful option.
Do you still suggest I edit the "en:" slot of the slug, 'commit', 'push' and proceed to a 'pull request'?
Many thanks, Roberto Scotti

@r-scotti So, the reason the other languages' definitions have the same sense as the English one is because (not always, but most often) entries in Glosario are defined in English first, and then translated into other languages. So, yes, I would still advise you to edit the English definition and commit, push, and submit a PR for that. You could also flag that the other definitions should be altered (for instance, in an issue), and people who are capable of doing so in those languages could be assigned to it, or could pick out those issues to work on.

As far as the cloning vs forking, I don't think there's a 'right' way to do it. It may just be the difference between whether there is a version of the repository in your GitHub account. I can't currently remember if cloning a repo you don't own, then pushing changes to it might somehow create a forked version in your account. At any rate, you don't have access to the main branch here, so if it goes directly to this repo, it will create a new, temporary branch.

Thank you for the clarification.

Is the model for the term `graph` what should be done?
```
 - slug: graph
  ref:
    - tree
  en:
    term: "graph"
    def: >
      1. A plot or a chart that displays data, or 2. a data structure in which
      [nodes](#node) are connected to one another by [edges](#edge).
```

Thanks for bringing this up. 

I think the format should be:

```
  en:
    term: "graph"
    def: >
      1. A plot or a chart that displays data.

      2. A data structure in which [nodes](#node) are connected to one
         another by [edges](#edge).
```

so we'll get a numbered list which would be more semantically correct and improve acceesibility.

👍 I am happy to work on that (particularly as I **have** actually read through the entire glossary, which I imagine is rare). And I have a list I started of terms that are overloaded. I would note though, that after going through the full thing, it seems like there is a different way some people have done this. Looking at the various feature_* entries, for example, you have: ``` - slug: feature_branch ref: - master_branch en: term: "feature branch" def: > A [branch](#branch) within a [Git](#git) [repository](#repository) containing [commits](#commit) dedicated to a specific feature, e.g., a [bug](#bug) fix or a new function. This branch can be merged into another branch. - slug: feature_data en: term: "feature (in data)" def: > A variable or observable in a dataset. - slug: feature_engineering en: term: "feature engineering" def: > The process of choosing the variables to be used as inputs to a [model](#model). Choosing good features often depends on [domain knowledge](#domain_knowledge). - slug: feature_request en: term: "feature request" def: > A request to the maintainers or developers of a software program to add a specific functionality (a feature) to that program. - slug: feature_software en: term: "feature (in software)" def: > Some aspect of software that was deliberately designed or built. A [bug](#bug) is an undesired feature. ``` I noticed this after creating this issue, but I think this method: 1. is confusing (because it is not immediately clear that these (some of these) define the term `feature` in different contexts (`feature_branch`, `feature_engineering`, and `feature_request` are actually terms, not a term + context)); 1. makes it harder to know where internal links should point (particularly if two entries are somewhat-similar; and 1. **does not reflect how actual dictionaries are written.** The numbering method you propose is ore in-line with the standard. I think the numbering system, with a way to specify context (see example image of different definitions of `run, *noun*`) is the way to go, but I imagine implementing this requires adding to the YAML parsing code used. (I haven't looked at this, yet, so don't even know what language it's written in.)

My immediate thought would be to cite existing definitions in a numbered style (i.e like Wikipedia).  Not sure how 'do-able' it is in Github and particularly the repo as it is now.

We could easily add a `sources` key with links and names. Should this be at the entry level or at the language level, i.e., would it be:

```
- slug: despondency
  sources:
  - name: "Wikipedia article on programming"
    url: http://something-or-other
  en:
    term: "depondency"
    def: "..."
  es:
    term: abatimiento
    def: "..."
```

or:

```
- slug: despondency
  en:
    term: "depondency"
    sources:
    - name: "Wikipedia article on programming"
      url: http://something-or-other
    def: "..."
  es:
    term: "abatimiento"
    # doesn't have external sources (yet)
    def: "..."
```

> We could easily add a `sources` key with links and names. Should this be at the entry level or at the language level

I would expect that sources would need to be at the language level since I am not likely to find a Polish translation of "Working Directory" in the same place as one in Farsi.

I believe so! [The reference guide is one of the older features of the lesson template](https://software-carpentry.org/blog/2014/10/new-lesson-template-v2.html), and they've been manually curated in the past, which leads to overlap in the definitions (e.g. Programming for Python and Programming for R both have the same definitions for loops), but also leads to divergence in definitions. 

One of the features I want to incorporate into the new template is to automatically create links to glosario (and/or compiling a local glossary reference).

Until the new template gains this feature, the current way to link to glosario would be to use: `[term](https://glosario.carpentries.org/lang/#term)`, so "libraries" in Spanish would be `[biblioteca](https://glosario.carpentries.org/es/#library)`: [biblioteca](https://glosario.carpentries.org/es/#library). If a definition does not exist in a given language, then the link will bring the user to the top of the page (which I realize now is not the best outcome since it's not clear that the definition does not exist).

Thanks @zkamvar! At this stage, I have no idea when our translated lesson is ready for publish, so, it is okay not to look good. However, I like the idea to insert a glosario link instead of reference.md. So, I will do that and go back to glosario to translate terms.

Another approach used by the folks over at the Jekyll lesson in the incubator is to just populate the glossary with the links to glosario: https://carpentries-incubator.github.io/jekyll-pages-novice/reference.html#glossary (instead of replacing all of the inline links).

The glossary (file/package) needs to include either a version number (or more readably, a publish timestamp) to make work fully reproducible.

An idea: if one of the points for this repo is a tool for teaching git, we could use the commit hash as the ID.  When the user asks to update the file, we [grab the latest commit hash from GitHub](https://docs.github.com/en/rest/reference/repos#get-a-commit) and compare it to the one the user has (this uses minimal bandwitdth). If it matches, we do nothing because they are up-to-date. If it doesn't match, then we download the new version and replace the hash. 

Because this is dynamically generated, we can just prepend the hash to the source file as a yaml entry, which also means that we can include a timestamp for that commit to improve readability.

@davidyakobovitch None of us have competency in all of the languages. I believe that usually the English entry comes first, and is then translated into other languages as people who speak them come along. Claim your entries and define them in whatever language(s) you are competent in. викидів -> значень?
Треба якось пояснити, що "квантиль" - це у загальному випадку, а для деяких кількостей груп їснують і спеціальні назви.
Мабуть треба пояснити, у якому випадку буде декілька значень?
це статистичний показник, що відображає, як змінюються дві величини одночасно. Якщо обидві зростають чи зменшуються разом - коваріація додатна, якщо одна зростає, а інша зменшується - від’ємна.

Коваріація використовується для виявлення зв’язку між змінними, але не нормується, тому не дозволяє точно оцінити силу цього зв’язку без додаткових перетворень, наприклад, розрахунку коефіцієнта кореляції.
Так, значень.

Квантиль - це загальний термін для значень, що ділять відсортований набір даних на рівні частини. При поділі на чотири групи використовують квартилі, на п’ять - квінтилі, на десять - децилі. Нижній квінтиль, наприклад, містить найнижчі 20 % значень.

Мода - одне чи декілька значень, які найчастіше зустрічаються в наборі даних. Якщо декілька значень мають однакову найвищу частоту, то набір має декілька мод, і такий розподіл називається мультимодальним.

"як двовимірну  (подібну до електронної таблиці або бази даних), яка містить рядки і стовпці." -> "двовимірну таблицю, подібну до електронної таблиці або бази даних, яка містить рядки та стовпці."

Використання дужок може створювати враження вставного речення, яке дещо вибивається із загального потоку тексту. Заміна дужок на коми інтегрує пояснення у речення, роблячи його більш плавним і зв'язним.

"Series можна уявити як одновимірний масив (список) з індексами, а DataFrame — як двовимірну таблицю, подібну до електронної таблиці або бази даних, яка містить рядки та стовпці." -> "Series представляє собою одновимірний масив (список) з індексами, а DataFrame — двовимірну таблицю, подібну до електронної таблиці або бази даних, з рядками та стовпцями." 

 This change simplifies the description by removing redundant words and making the sentence more direct. "представляє собою" is a more formal and precise way of describing the concept than "можна уявити як".

Neither is optimal: https://www.doi.org/the-identifier/what-is-a-doi/ says "A DOI is a digital identifier of an object, any object — physical, digital, or abstract. DOIs solve a common problem: keeping track of things. Things can be matter, material, content, or activities."

Thus, it is a "digital identifier" of an "object", and not an "identifier" of a "digital object":

    (Digital Object) Identifier != Digital (Object Identifier)

You have conflicts with the main branch. This probably happened because your current branch is not up to date with the main Glosario branch. To resolve it, you need to:

Run `git remote -v` to check which remotes you have. In my case, I have:
```Git
origin    git@github.com:Dod900ls1/glosario.git (fetch)
origin    git@github.com:Dod900ls1/glosario.git (push)
upstream  git@github.com:carpentries/glosario.git (fetch)
upstream  git@github.com:carpentries/glosario.git (push)
```
Then, you need to rebase on the original remote `carpentries/glosario.git`. In this case, the original remote is called upstream, so you would write:
`git rebase upstream/main`

After you do that, just write `git push -f` to upload your changes. Check if you have any more conflicts. If so, I can help you resolve them.

Another  option would be to add by agreement one or more of @VeronikaShevc @YehorBoiar @Herobread to your fork as collaborators (see https://ukrainian-carpentries.github.io/git-novice/08-collab.html for instructions) and then they can push new commits to your branch and update your PR. Edit it, commit changes, and the pull request will be automatically updated with the new version of the file. You can write something like "Fix indentation" etc. in the commit message.

When I imagine an agile methodology, I always think of this picture. 

![image](https://github.com/carpentries/glosario/assets/94189697/88271558-faee-4917-8961-2ad60d00f02a)

Also, I did some research on agile methodology, and all papers that I have found use the word "iteration" rather than "change" or similar. Here is a paper that I found the most topical:

- [Adoption_of_agile_methodology_in_software_development.pdf](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/15951635/Adoption_of_agile_methodology_in_software_development.pdf) [2]

Paper has a clear description of what agile methodology is on page 2, and it uses the word iteration. 

Therefore, I think that the word "ітерація" would be better.
interesting, I agree with `ітерація` as it is quite popular in math and CS, but at the same time, if you choose by meaning of it in vocabulary, then `зміна` is better

Well, they have taken this term from the "Гірничий енциклопедичний словник" that had its last update in 2004, so I don't think that the term "ітерація" is up to date. In the [cambrige dictionary](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/iteration)  it has 2 definitions:
- "the [process](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/process) of doing something again and again, usually to [improve](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/improve) it, or one of the [times](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/times) you do it"
- "an [amount](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/amount) that you get when you use a [mathematical](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/mathematical) [rule](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/rule) several [times](https://dictionary.cambridge.org/us/dictionary/english/times)"

And I think that we could use the word iteration in the first meaning of its word according to cambrige dictionary, and here are some papers that actually do that:
- https://nzlubp.org.ua/index.php/journal/article/view/400/377 [page 2]
- http://dspace.wunu.edu.ua/bitstream/316497/42578/1/%d0%91%d0%b0%d0%bb%d0%b0%d0%b7%d1%8e%d0%ba.pdf [page 3]
- https://archive.mcnd.org.ua/index.php/conference-proceeding/article/view/980/1000 [page 2]

So, "ітерація" is a better word.

@ccoVeille sorry for lots of notifications - we have a translation sprint in June-July, so there may be quite a few more coming shortly. Please note this if you'd like to adjust your watching preferences for these two months. The repository has both examples of PR for a single word and for multiple terms, and Glosario maintainers are happy with us doing it that way. OTOH, we plan to review and merge PRs quickly, so they will disappear from the list of open PRs, reducing the noise.

One thought that I have against mixing many words in one PR - if you have commit A for word X, and then later commit B for word Y, you can't fix the commit A by `--amend` anymore, as it's not the latest. You can of course, use `git rebase -i` to refactor the history, and rewrite older commits, but that require more advanced skills - not every contributor is well versed with these commands yet...

Maybe. Although it may be nice to translate "legacy system" to Ukrainian too, maybe in a separate article.

I do have indeed some reservations against saying "with an older legacy system" since it does not have to be a legacy one. E.g. I tend to understand legacy code as something that no longer supported, or is very much outdated etc. But if I just made a new release X.Y+1 of my software, and added a new optional keyword argument for a Python function A, it will be backwards compatible with code written for version X.Y not using that keyword argument. That does not make version X.Y a legacy one, I think.

Maybe this something to consider for English version too (CC @elletjies and @froggleston)? E.g. saying "A property of a system, hardware or software, that allows for interoperability with an older system" without the word "legacy", or "... with an older version of the system or even another legacy system".

`Стандарт, який визначає числові коди для багатьох тисяч символів і знаків. Unicode не визначає, як ці числа зберігаються; це робиться за допомогою таких стандартів, як [UTF-8](#utf_8).`

I don't think so. I think even though my version is a little bit longer, it contains much more insight into the term. In particular, I don't like those parts:

- "коди для багатьох тисяч символів і знаків" - It doesn't tell what symbols it assigns the code to, and it doesn't mention that this code has to be unique.
- It doesn't tell that this standard is constantly updating, unlike, for example, the ASCII or UTF-8 standards, which is essential.

However, I do think that it would be the best if it was shorter. I'm open to any suggestions in this direction!

Good  question, leading to a conceptual discussion (and possibly to feeding back @Dod900ls1's additions to the English version). English version is usually a good start. but we do not have to do a word-by-word translation. If the exact translation is good, then it's fine. But if you find out that it does not express the concept well enough, or that the concept is better explained in Ukrainian sources that you have used, by means of some other formulations, consider other options too, trying to follow the concise style of Glosario.

In this particular example, @Dod900ls1's text is still short enough, and adds useful context, helpful for understanding - I would be happy to accept it.

Hello again... I am new to this Glossary thing... It think it is a great idea.

This is a very difficult translation as "exactitude" and "precision" are often used interchangeably.     
I don't speak German, but I used their translation [Genauigkeit](https://translate.google.com/?sl=auto&tl=en&text=Genauigkeit&op=translate) in Google Translate and both "exactitude" and "precision" are suggested translations amongst more words.

This web https://asana.com/resources/accuracy-vs-precision has a good explanation of both terms.

```
Accuracy evaluates how close you are to the true value of the measurement, 
while precision shows how close the measured values are to one another. 
```
The (original language?) English is about *accuracy* and **not about** *precision*. 

AT FIRST I thought that using *exactitude* was not the correct word for the French translation because *precision* was used in the other languages that I can understand (*e.g.* Spanish.) However, the above definition as `Accuracy evaluates how close you are to the true value of the measurement` suggests that SOME of the other language translations are not reflecting the correct translation of the English `accuracy`.

Since "exactitude" is a feminine word in French, your definition should be slightly altered when providing the formula from "Il" to "Elle" and also adding the feminine "e" ending to the calculation word "calculé***e***."

 >l'exactitude est la proximité des mesures à une valeur spécifique, tandis que la précision est la proximité des mesures les unes par rapport aux autres. ***Elle*** est calculé***e*** comme `Exactitude = (TP+TN)/(TP+TN+FP+FN)`

This French definition therefore does not contain a translation of the English, as used by the other langages, but does contain the contrast definition between the 2 words, which is useful.
Thank you @jsgro! Can you help me with a full definition for this translation (as you suggested above). I can then add the changes in GitHub?

Greetings Angelique. I see that you are the author of the page at https://carpentries.org/blog/2024/02/Glosario-Codefest/ and there will be a "Glosario Codefest" tomorrow (May 15) it seems, and the Univ. of Wisconsin-Madison (USA) will participate. Trying to understand how the whole thing works I stumbled on your earlier review and thought that this would be a rather easy definition. But in fact it's not!

The first problem is that the English version is definitely not the best, as it is not a broad definition but a definition based on "statistical models" and related to what is called a "Confusion Matrix" as it is explained in this post: [Confusion Matrix to No Confusion Matrix in Just 5mins](https://pub.towardsai.net/confusion-matrix-179b9c758b55)
and also present the following formula 

`Accuracy = (TP+TN)/(TP+FP+FN+TN)` but the [Carpentry glossary](https://github.com/carpentries/glosario/blob/main/glossary.yml) definition of "Accuracy" does not specify what these letters mean:

- TP = True Positive
- FP = False Positive
- FN = False Negative
- TN = True Negative

As these terms are NOT defined in the original English proposition, the meaning of the formula becomes moot and senseless! and are unfortunately blindly copied in all other language translations.

Since the English definition is *biased* on the general idea of a "Statistical model," all other languages have simply translated the same bias... I think it's not the best approach for this particular word. This *bias* of definition is likely due to the fact that some author of a lesson wanted to define *Accuracy* for that particular lesson, but it is not a general definition.

Since we can only alter the French defintion, I suggest that we make use of the simpler definition I mentioned previously on this web page:  https://asana.com/resources/accuracy-vs-precision 

```
Accuracy and precision are both ways to measure results. Accuracy measures how close results are 
to the true or known value. Precision, on the other hand, measures how close results are to one another. 
```

Which translate in French as follows, and we can also add: *Accuracy = Exactitude* to clarify the specific translation of that word:


```
Accuracy = Exactitude
L'exactitude et la précision sont deux façons de mesurer les résultats. 
L'exactitude mesure à quel point les résultats sont proches de la valeur vraie ou connue. 
La précision, quant à elle, mesure la proximité des résultats les uns par rapport aux autres. 
```

The advantage of this definition is:

1. It is very clear, without jargon or formula
2. It offers a specific definition for both accuracy and precision which are often used interchangeably but are not the same.
3. It is not dependent on any "Model" or "Statistical Model"

I would even strongly suggest that this should be the basis of the definition of *Accuracy* in all other languages.

Best wishes,
JYS

```suggestion
      Conjunto de [licencias](#license) que pueden ser aplicadas a trabajos publicados. Cada licencia está formada por la concatenación de uno o más de: -BY (Atribución): se debe citar la fuente original; -SA (CompartirIgual): se debe compartir el trabajo bajo una licencia similar; -NC (NoComercial): el trabajo no se puede usar con fines comerciales sin la autorización de las personas creadoras; -ND (NoDerivaciones): no se pueden crear trabajos derivados (por ejemplo, traducciones) sin el permiso de las personas autoras. Así, CC-BY-NC significa "se debe atribuir el trabajo a las personas creadoras y no se puede utilizar comercialmente sin permiso". El término CC-0 (cero, no letra O), se utiliza a menudo para referirse a "sin restricciones", en el que el trabajo es de dominio público.
```
I will suggest using the Spanish version we can found on the official website of Creative Commons.  I don't think we are doing a better job explaining this here.

```suggestion
      In informatica, un algoritmo è una sequenza finita di istruzioni presenti in un programma che permette di risolvere un problema computazionale.
```

Un insieme è una collezione non ordinata, mentre in un algoritmo è importante che le instruzioni vengano eseguite nell'ordine specificato. Credo "sequenza ordinata" descriva meglio la definizione.

```suggestion
      Un algoritmo è una sequenza finita di operazioni da eseguire al fine di realizzare un certo compito.
```

Non penso serva parlare di regole, poiché un algoritmo non può semplicemente specificare cosa fare senza descrivere come, altrimenti non sarebbe eseguibile.

Ad esempio,
regola: non si possono accettare liste vuote
istruzione: controllare se la lista è vuota e lanciare un'eccezione in caso affermativo

I notice that there are 2 definitions of "algorithm" in German now:

"      Ein Algorithmus ist in der Mathematik und der IT eine Vorgehensweise um
      Probleme zu lösen. Ein Algorithmus ist ein schrittweiser, eindeutiger
      Plan, oft in viele kleine Abschnitte unterteilt und mit Querverweisen
      ausgestattet, um ein spezifisches Problem zu lösen."

and

"Ein Algorithmus beschreibt eine Handlungsvorschrift, ein Verfahren oder einen Regelsatz zum Lösen eines Problems. In der Informatik ist ein Algorithmus eine eindeutige, ausführbare Folge von Anweisungen endlicher Länge zur Lösung eines Problems, welche immer das gleiche Ergebnis liefern."

Which one should we keep?

Thanks!

Hi Brian,

Thanks for your contribution. The term you introduce is ableist language. Several projects are moving away from using such terms in their code (e.g., [Android](https://source.android.com/setup/contribute/respectful-code), [Twitter](https://twitter.com/TwitterEng/status/1278733305190342656)). I defer to the @carpentries/glosario-editors-en to address this. A possible way could be that the term gets included in the glossary but the definition should mention that it is ableist and links to alternative terms.

hups, sorry for that :D for what is worth, it seems to me that most of the other markdown files also use `layout` 🤔

We use both. The confusing aspect here is that `ltr` is the default and is omitted in most places, while it's required on `rtl` pages (e.g., https://github.com/carpentries/glosario/blob/master/ar.md#L4). I'll update the yaml headers to include the `direction` entry everywhere and update the docs accordingly.

> We use both. The confusing aspect here is that `ltr` is the default and is omitted in most places, while it's required on `rtl` pages (e.g., https://github.com/carpentries/glosario/blob/master/ar.md#L4). I'll update the yaml headers to include the `direction` entry everywhere and update the docs accordingly. I think ultimately the confusing aspect is the scope of these yaml items in Jekyll for those who are not familiar with Jekyll: 1. The `layout` item is a jekyll-specific item that tells Jekyll which template in `_layouts` needs to be used and is required 2. The `direction` item is a glosario-specific item that allows us to parse what direction we display the definitions in language-specific pages. The reason we have two different layouts is because the handling of the layout between pages is, well, different. At the moment, here are the diffs between the pages:
git diff --no-index -- _layouts/glossary-{ltr,rtl}.html ```diff diff --git a/_layouts/glossary-ltr.html b/_layouts/glossary-rtl.html index bf43c7b..89c7ee2 100644 --- a/_layouts/glossary-ltr.html +++ b/_layouts/glossary-rtl.html @@ -1,30 +1,65 @@ --- --- + {%- if page.title -%} + {%- assign the_title = page.title -%} + {%- else -%} + {%- assign language = page.permalink | replace: '/', '' %} + {%- assign the_title = site.languages | where: "key", language | map: "title" | first -%} + {%- assign the_name = site.languages | where: "key", language | map: "name" | first -%} + {%- endif -%} {{site.title}} - - - - + + +
- {% include title.html %} + +
+
+
+ + + + View project onGitHub + +
+
+ +

{{the_title}}

+
+

{{ the_name | default: site.description }}

I'm going to cite O'Reilly here, in their [definition of data engineering](https://www.oreilly.com/content/data-engineering-a-quick-and-simple-definition/#:~:text=Data%20engineers%20make%20sure%20the,use%20cases%20may%20present%20themselves.&text=Data%20wrangling%20is%20about%20taking,turning%20it%20into%20something%20useful.) they say this:

> Data engineers wrangle data into a state that can then have queries run against it by data scientists.

I would suggest the definition be something like this:
```suggestion
      Another name for small-scale [data engineering](#data_engineering).
```
or:
```suggestion
      Some of the work done by [data engineers](#data_engineer).
```

```suggestion
### Building a local test site (optional)
*N.B. this is not a required step for submitting a PR to Glosario.*

Once you have finished creating your glossary entry it may be useful to build a local test site to check the syntax and formatting is correct. To do this you will need a working installation of Jekyll on your local machine (instructions for which can be found [here](https://jekyllrb.com/docs/installation/).
```

@bjthorpe 

I understand the intention here, and I think providing a bit more information about how one might test the site is good. However, I'm concerned that this wording might make people feel as if they are expected to test the deployment before they submit a PR. We have been trying to make this project more inclusive and welcoming to people who may not know a lot about GitHub, local deployment, or other things. The submission of new entries offers a way to begin contributing to open-source projects that doesn't require a lot of previous experience.

As the person who is generally looking in on PRs to see why builds fail, I can attest that the error messages thrown up by the linter and other things can be quite arcane, and I do not want to place responsibility for that on newcomers. I do, however, let them know why things are failing, so that when they (hopefully) contribute again, they understand how to avoid the same errors.

```suggestion
With this, you can then use the command `make serve` this will create a local copy of the GitHub pages site that you can view in a web browser. The IP address for this is listed in the command line output as `Server address: http://X.Y.Z:4000` however the default address is `http://localhost:4000`.
```

```suggestion
Another useful command is `make check` which checks the syntax for the glossary is correct without actually creating the GitHub pages site.
```

Suggestion: プログラム内で使用される、データが関連付けられる箱の総称。変数の値は定義した後で変更が可能。

I understand this sentence is specifically added for Japanese. If it should stay, I would suggest to modify:
変数は英語で「variable」(ヴァリアブル)という / 呼ぶ (whichever you prefer)。

Not sure about the scope of glosario but I've seen a lot of Japanese definitions give the English term, especially for technical jargon as Japanese users will likely encounter them in English documentation.

Change to plain form as suggested.

```suggestion
      変数は英語で「variable」(ヴァリアブル)という。
```

I get what they're going for with the 箱(box) metaphor to store the data. It's an interesting idea since it's a hard concept to explain and takes a long time for beginner's to understand. I've removed it for consistency with other languages. e.g., 

```diff
-    A name for box in a program that has some data stored in it. 
+    A name in a program that has some data associated with it. 
```

```diff
-    プログラム内で使用される、データが関連付けられる箱の総称。
+    プログラム内で使用される、データが関連付けられる総称。
```

Otherwise I agree to the suggestions. Your second sentence is nice and concise.

```suggestion
      プログラム内で使用される、データが関連付けられる総称。変数の値は定義した後で変更が可能。
```

We need an object before '総称', e.g., 箱 or 値. If we add extra information like '変数は英語で「variable」(ヴァリアブル)という', it may be okay to use 'a box' there. Anyhow, I will ask others' thoughts on Slack and go from there!

Good idea. I'm open to a non-literal translation if it aids understanding. In this case, 箱(box) is more appropriate that 値 (value) I think. So the "variable" is a the name of the box which contains the data or value.

```r
name <- "data"
```

On further reading, I agree that 総称 is better than 名前.

```suggestion
      プログラム内で使用される、データが関連付けられる箱の総称。変数の値は定義した後で変更が可能。
```

Probably we can add "のようなもの" after "箱" so that it is clearer that this is a sort of abstract expression rather than concrete thing?
```suggestion
      プログラム内で使用される、データが関連付けられる箱のようなものの総称。変数の値は定義した後で変更が可能。
```

Unless we decide to remove English term for 'variable' and ['vector'](https://github.com/carpentries/glosario/pull/303#discussion_r613628221)?

@baileythegreen Thanks for the feedback. I was just typing that question and figured it out as you posted. I will work on the definition to include your suggestions.

Thank you for your contribution, @leedotson. Since this PR is over a year old, I am going to close this. Feel free to reopen with the requested changes. 

I agree with @baileythegreen that the scope of this definition is too broad for the term "keyword." Specifically, a keyword does not necessarily need to be a reserved word (as Bailey described with python's named parameters (aka keyword arguments)).
@all-contributors please add @leedotson for translation

```suggestion
      A word which has a special meaning in a programming language and therefore cannot be
      used as a variable name, a [function](#function) name, or other identifier. Keywords can
      be [commands](#command) or [parameters](#parameter). Also known as reserved names
      or reserved words.
```

I am not sure the examples here (for what keywords can be) are complete, or accurate.

**Accuracy**
For example, a *keyword parameter* is not, actually, a keyword in the sense of reserved words; it is, in fact, a named parameter, which has an identifier that can not be a keyword (although the named parameter's value could be a keyword, such as `True` or `False`). I am currently unable to think of a situation where a parameter can be a keyword (as opposed to the parameter's value being a keyword), so if you do have an example, please share it. (And if my point is not clear, please tell me; it's been a long day.)

**Completeness**
Reserved words are generally commands, statements, primitive data types (`True`, `False`), ones that indicate how a particular block of code should be interpreted (like `for`, `while`, `if`, `else`, whatever signals a function/method/class definition). Right now the definition doesn't cover all of those.

```suggestion
      Human-readable text written in a programming language 
      with the goal of setting exact rules for a computer-executable application.
```

I don't agree with the

> goal of setting exact rules for

bit; that sounds more like it refers to parameter settings than source code as a whole. The concept of source code as a series of actions is probably more useful. Also, the definition should reference the difference between source code and compiled code explicitly; likely in a second sentence. Right now, this is only implied by the words 'human-readable'.

Also, to explain the deletions here:

> created by a programmer

This potentially comes across as gate-keeping, because people might write some code without really considering themselves programmers. It also implies source code can only be written by a human, but the entire purpose of some programs (transpilers, or 'source-to-source compilers) is to take source code in one language and produce source code in another language.

I removed the word 'specific' because it felt unnecessary, and could also be seen to imply (erroneously, but...) that code not written in one of a set of languages can not be source code.

Saying this again because I see no changes that address it:

I don't agree with the

> goal of setting exact rules for

bit; that sounds more like it refers to parameter settings than source code as a whole. The concept of source code as a series of actions is probably more useful. Also, the definition should reference the difference between source code and compiled code explicitly; likely in a second sentence. Right now, this is only implied by the words 'human-readable'.

Thanks for this! This is a particularly broad thing to try to define, but an important one. After consultation on your proposed definition with a CS professor, I'm requesting some changes. They keep the sense of your definition, but make it less concrete, so it more accurately describes a greater proportion of all possible data structures.

I've also changed it to British spellings (preferred in this project) and added an internal link.

The addition looks good to me, particularly as I almost submitted the exact same thing the other day....

As for a style guide, it's a good idea, but I don't know of one. If there is one, it's been hidden very well, I think. The Contributing Guidelines focus more on YAML syntax/checking the build of the site than anything language-specific.

Given the number of conversations the Japanese editors have had recently on style, a guide would seem to be a useful thing; there may be other languages that could benefit from it, for instance, languages with gendered nouns, where we might set guidelines on whether specific conventions should be used in that language's definitions to make them gender-neutral (at least with respect to references to people).

We may also want to avoid use of some [computer terms](https://t.co/GQbKUFDVCx?amp=1) to be more inclusive.

This seems a perfect translation of the English definition here in glossary.yml. However, Given the first paragraph of INTRODUCTION of ['A new approach to clustering' (1969, Ruspini)](https://doi.org/10.1016/S0019-9958(69)90591-9) on p. 23,  I feel the English definition here misses information about how the grouping is done as a result (but not "how" as means, if this makes sense). So IMO, the English definition should be something like "The process of dividing data into groups when the groups themselves are not known in advance, but so that as a result data points within a group are more similar to each other than than data points in the other groups in certain perspective."

I am not an expert of this field, so I maybe misunderstanding. I felt like the current definition feels like "grouping data in whatever way, could be random". So if this is the case, I think again the translation is perfect.
Awesome! Thanks @masamiy :)

Perhaps I can help here? Clustering is used a lot in my field.

You're right that the result of clustering is to group similar data points together but the "how" complicates things. There are many ways to perform clustering (hierarchical, graph-based, k-means, etc) so it's difficult to generalise and I think not necessarily helpful to novice users to go into detail. I agree that the English definition is very simple but I think it is okay.

```suggestion
      by defining a set of tasks that takes zero or more required and optional arguments as inputs and return expected outputs (return values), if any.
```

Elsewhere in this glossary we (read: I) have defined all of the input values passed to a function as arguments, which can be either required or optional, and with the understanding that a function can take required arguments, optional arguments, a mixture of the two, or no arguments at all. Consequently, this wording in English feels inconsistent.

Return values are also optional in some languages (Python, for example, has an optional `return` statement, and will implicitly return `None` if no `return` statement or return value are given).

@zkamvar, @gvwilson  Here is the comment I originally left regarding [a REPL/an REPL] and why the second is correct. It is a difference between the word (acronym, in this case) starting with a **vowel sound** versus starting with a **vowel**. (I think all words that start with a vowel also start with a vowel sound, because I can't think of any counterexamples; however, not all words that start with a vowel sound start with a vowel. I am uncertain as to the size of that list, though.)

#### Original comment
I understand why you would think this, but because the actual pronunciation of the letter 'R' starts with a vowel sound, it needs the 'n'.

(If you say R-E-P-L, the first syllable is the same as the first syllable of argument, and you say an argument.

I say this as a user of Vim for the past three years: I think it is definitely worth keeping some sense of how difficult it is for newcomers to use Vim.

I think R may be an exception here. I don't know, I don't use it. And, to be clear, I am saying assertions should not be used for **data validation** in production code. Using them for testing is fine, but in production code they should only identify things that truly break code. *There are other ways to validate data.*

I feel very strongly about this. [Here is a good summary of why; see Caveat #1: Don't Use Asserts for Data Validation](https://dbader.org/blog/python-assert-tutorial).

Python, and several other languages have an option to optimise assertions away. (I consulted an actual computer science professor on that point when I was correcting this entry, just to make sure this wasn't the minority case.) In Python, assertions are on by default, but turned off under any optimisation condition, but in Java, this is the other way around—assertions must be explicitly enabled (source, Deitel & Deitel 10th edition, Java: How to Program). But, basically, if you rely on data validation to verify the output of a program, and that data validation can be disabled, that seems like really bad practice, particularly if anyone else uses the code and that point isn't very clearly communicated.

There is also a Python lesson that also teaches assertions, and advocates using them for data validation, but that doesn't make it right. I have submitted an [issue](https://github.com/swcarpentry/python-novice-inflammation/issues/874) about it, talked to the maintainers in the issue comments, and am working on a pull request to fix it.

I would be willing to compromise with something citing R as an exception, or saying that 'In many languages, assertions should not be used...,' but I think the point is important to mention.

That's what I tried to type, but it wasn't working—my keyboard + vim didn't want to do them. But it looked like the colons were wreaking havoc with the syntax highlighting, and I thought that might pose other problems.

Kind of a difficult question. In Python `named arguments` are not a thing, but `keyword arguments` are, and they must be named. I am unsure if this is simply a difference in terminology, or if there are some languages that have both `named arguments` and `keyword arguments`.

I put it here, because this is the entry for `variable (numbers of) arguments`, and `**kwargs` is how a variable number of `keyword arguments` is listed in a Python function definition.

If `**kwargs` needs to be mentioned in `keyword arguments` then, by the same logic, `**args` needs to be mentioned in the entry for `argument`.

After reading the post, I think R is indeed an exception because R does not have the concept of an assert statement in the same sense as other languages since there is no way to ignore these commands with a flag to the interpreter.

R is a bit of an odd language probably because it was built by statisticians. I think it's safe to make a note citing R as an exception to the rule or even saying that R does not have an assert statement.
Agreed that it is a difficult question. I'm fine to leave this as-is until someone can come up with a clean solution.

I think the issue comes from formatting of the markdown links. A valid markdown link must have a square bracket and the opposite parenthesis on the same line

For example, these three markdown links:

```markdown
1. [glosario](https://glosario.carpentries.org)
2. [glosario
](
https://glosario.carpentries.org
)
3. [glosario]
(https://glosario.carpentries.org)
```

1. [glosario](https://glosario.carpentries.org)
2. [glosario
](
https://glosario.carpentries.org)
3. [glosario]
(https://glosario.carpentries.org)

> I was afraid to commit to `carpentries:master` before I could see if the check is OK, but I looks like it in was `marcosvital:master`.
> The checks are ok now, I'll try to merge it :)

This is a common pattern that reflects on the historically poor documentation for [GitHub Flow](https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/) (which only describes the branching aspect, but doesn't delve into the forking aspect. The [Happy Git With R tutorial](https://happygitwithr.com/fork-and-clone.html#fork-and-clone) by Dr. Jenny Bryan does a REALLY good job of introducing this clearly, but it's definitely focused on R users. 

If you try to edit the file directly on github, you will often find that it will automatically create a fork and new branch for you, which is helpful, but kind of obfuscates the process.

This is all to say, thank you @marcosvital for you contribution and thank you @beatrizmilz for handling the merge conflict (which threw more than one curvball!)

> Hi @zkamvar
> I did as you suggested in #114 and made a big bonfire out of it. Now I'm back again.
> Could you perhaps tell me what the proper way of doing this is. (If there is such a thing). Per the instructions, I created the fork, cloned it, created a new branch, made the edits, add, commit and pushed it and then created a new PR.
> What should the next steps be if I want to work on the next edit?

That's a good question and something that I think needs to be added to the CONTRIBUTING document. 

The next steps are detailed in https://happygitwithr.com/upstream-changes.html#upstream-changes:

 1. make sure you have carpentries/glosario as your "upstream" repository
 1. make sure you are on the master branch of your repository
 1. use `git pull upstream master --ff-only`to update that branch
 1. check out a new branch, add your contribution, and create a new pull request

Hello @jsteyn,

It looks like there may have been a mix-up with branches on your repository and this PR accidentally deletes an item that was added in #100. 

Looking at your fork, it appears that your master branch has some extra commits beyond this repository, so I don't think there's a good way to do this in this particular PR.

My suggestion is the [burn it all down](https://happygitwithr.com/burn.html) approach. In short:

1. delete your fork of this repository from github and your computer
2. re-fork the repository and clone to your computer
3. create a new branch
4. re-add your changes

This is not an uncommon situation to be in (I've done this many times), so you're in good company :)
Do let me know if you would like any help with this.
Thank you!

Please specify the language and terms in the title of the issue and/or pull request, e.g., `Romanian: logistic regression`.

Having said that, I suspect people will find this onerous: if I want to add a single term, I'm not going to file the issue and then write the PR - I'm just going to write the PR. Can we ask them to put language/term in the subject of the PR and then also ask contributors to search those as well as issues before contributing? Issues can then be used if people are planning to write multiple definitions, e.g., "Romanian/relational database terms".

Again, I suspect this is too heavyweight: my experience is that I usually need to add 2-4 terms at a time because (for example) "anti-join" and "outer join" depend on "join". Creating three PRs would be possible in this case, but one-per-term would _not_ work in the case where definitions refer to each other: if A references B and B references A and they're both new, a single-term PR will fail a glossary check.
indentation
Note that the keys like `term` and `acronym` are _not_ to be translated: they must be as-is. (We've had at least one person use the Spanish word for 'term'.)

The thinking behind this was to reduce redundancy of effort if many people want to contribute simultaneously (as could be the case during Hacktoberfest): it can be frustrating to spend time working on something only to find someone else was doing exactly the same thing. (This has happened to me before.) Although I hope this project attracts many contributions far into the future, I doubt such measures will be necessary permanently.

> Again, I suspect this is too heavyweight: my experience is that I usually need to add 2-4 terms at a time because (for example) "anti-join" and "outer join" depend on "join". Creating three PRs would be possible in this case, but one-per-term would _not_ work in the case where definitions refer to each other: if A references B and B references A and they're both new, a single-term PR will fail a glossary check.

I like this proposal of Martha's Rules. It's a bit like a pared down version of the [fist-to-five](https://www.lucidmeetings.com/glossary/fist-five) method (steps 2-4) for measuring consensus that we used in my grad union.

I think this is a good standard for our current group size, but the time limit here does present a barrier for those who are not able to communicate fluently (e.g. those who have disabilities related to verbal communication and/or are not relatively comfortable speaking in English). I would suggest to use a modification from the text for Martha's rules of something like "during roll call, members will state how much time they reasonably require for comments. For example, an abled English speaker may request 2 minutes as reasonable." This way, we can be equitable for any members who require more time to speak.

this action takes care of copying the `glossary.yml` file from the root folder to the `_data` folder (where Jekyll expects it). Then it copies the entire content of the repository into a new folder that gets imported into the `gh-pages` branch. From there, we rely on GitHub pages to generate the site.
 
This last part (pushing to the `gh-pages`) can only be done with the correct PAT. When others work on their forks they would see a failure at this step if we don't add this check because they wouldn't be able to push to the `gh-pages` branch. Does that make sense?

Yeah, this is going to be a problem... `make serve` copies `./glossary.yml` into `./_data/glossary.yml` and then runs `bundle exec jekyll serve` to build and serve the site, so if you preview using the Makefile, everything is OK (see #17).
